title: Choose a visual direction on explicit axes
principle: Define direction using audience-relevant axes such as dense–spacious, quiet–expressive, editorial–instrumental, and familiar–novel.
rationale: Named axes turn taste into a reviewable decision and prevent arbitrary blending of unrelated references.
applies_when: Establishing or revising the visual language of a page or product.
exceptions: A mature system may inherit its axes, but deviations still require a reason.
implementation: Select a target position and a deliberate range on each relevant axis.
Tie each choice to audience, content, and interaction density.
verification: Two reviewers can describe the intended direction consistently.
Major styling choices can be traced to an axis rather than personal preference.
accessibility: Expressiveness never reduces contrast, focus visibility, or comprehension.
responsive: Direction survives reflow instead of depending on one desktop composition.
performance: Media and effects required by the direction fit the page budget.

title: Decompose references before borrowing
principle: Extract reference decisions—hierarchy, rhythm, geometry, type, color roles, imagery, and motion—rather than copying surface decoration.
rationale: Decomposition preserves useful intent while avoiding incoherent imitation and helps distinguish brand signal from incidental screenshot conditions.
applies_when: A screenshot, competitor, moodboard, or design-system example informs the work.
exceptions: Pixel-accurate reconstruction may reproduce surface details when the user explicitly requires it and rights permit.
implementation: Annotate each reference by structural and stylistic layers.
Record what to adopt, translate, and reject with reasons.
verification: The result remains coherent after replacing reference-specific content.
Borrowed decisions serve the new product brief.
accessibility: Reject reference behavior that hides focus, weakens contrast, or depends on pointer precision.
responsive: Infer rules from multiple widths; do not treat one screenshot as the responsive specification.
performance: Identify costly reference effects and choose a budget-aware equivalent.
browser_notes: Separate browser-rendering artifacts from intentional design.

title: Create distinctiveness through a repeatable system
principle: Concentrate personality in two or three repeatable signals—such as type contrast, shape language, image treatment, or motion cadence—while keeping utility patterns familiar.
rationale: A small expressive grammar feels authored and scalable; novelty on every surface feels chaotic and harms learnability.
applies_when: The product needs recognizable character without sacrificing task clarity.
exceptions: Highly regulated or emergency contexts may intentionally minimize expressive signals.
implementation: Name the signature signals and encode them as tokens or documented recipes.
Apply them at high-value moments, not indiscriminately.
verification: A new page can use the signals without inventing new decoration.
Removing one signal does not collapse usability.
accessibility: Brand signals have non-visual equivalents where they communicate state.
responsive: Signals adapt or simplify at constrained sizes without disappearing entirely.
performance: Prefer CSS, type, and reusable assets over repeated heavyweight effects.
browser_notes: Define a graceful baseline for unsupported visual capabilities.

title: Let content determine the composition
principle: Choose composition from content relationships and reading sequence; do not force every message into a prefabricated landing-page stack.
rationale: Content-led composition creates hierarchy and avoids the interchangeable appearance of template-driven interfaces.
applies_when: Organizing page sections, hero content, proof, comparisons, or editorial material.
exceptions: A standardized transaction flow may deliberately prioritize consistency over expressive composition.
implementation: Rank content by user decision value.
Select layout structures that express those relationships.
verification: The page remains understandable in a text outline.
Section boundaries correspond to meaning rather than alternating background decoration.
accessibility: Reading and heading order remain logical independent of visual placement.
responsive: The intended narrative survives single-column reflow.
performance: Critical content is not delayed behind ornamental media.
browser_notes: CSS visual reordering does not contradict DOM reading order.

title: Encode meaning in design tokens
principle: Components consume semantic roles such as surface, text, border, focus, danger, and spacing intent rather than raw brand values.
rationale: Semantic tokens allow themes, modes, contrast adjustments, and product evolution without scattering meaning across components.
applies_when: More than one component shares visual decisions or theming is possible.
exceptions: A one-off data visualization may need carefully documented local scales.
implementation: Separate primitive scales from semantic aliases and component tokens.
Document fallback chains and mode overrides.
verification: Changing a semantic role updates all intended consumers and no unrelated consumers.
Components contain no unexplained raw color or spacing literals.
accessibility: Contrast-critical roles are tested in every theme and state.
responsive: Use tokens for density and spacing changes without tying meaning to one viewport.
performance: Ship only used token modes and avoid runtime style churn.
browser_notes: Provide fallbacks for advanced custom-property features required by the support baseline.

title: Define component contracts before variants
principle: Specify purpose, anatomy, content limits, states, behaviors, accessibility semantics, and extension points before adding visual variants.
rationale: Variant-first components accumulate combinatorial APIs and inconsistent behavior while leaving actual usage constraints implicit.
applies_when: Creating or materially revising a reusable component.
exceptions: A private one-use composition can remain local until a second stable use appears.
implementation: Write a compact contract and state matrix.
Prefer composable slots and meaningful props over boolean combinations.
verification: Unsupported prop combinations are impossible or documented.
Examples cover default, boundary, loading, empty, error, and disabled behavior where relevant.
accessibility: Semantic role, name, focus, keyboard interaction, and announcements are part of the contract.
responsive: Contract states how content wraps, truncates, or recomposes.
performance: Contract records rendering, hydration, and asset expectations for heavy components.
browser_notes: Native-element behavior and browser differences are documented before abstraction.

title: Design the adoption path with the system
principle: Provide migration recipes, codemods where appropriate, deprecation policy, examples, and an escape hatch alongside new system primitives.
rationale: A technically elegant system fails when adoption requires risky rewrites or teams cannot express legitimate edge cases.
applies_when: Introducing shared foundations into an existing product or changing a public component API.
exceptions: A greenfield product still needs examples and governance but may not need migration tooling.
implementation: Publish incremental migration steps and compatibility windows.
Track exceptions to learn which primitives are missing.
verification: A representative legacy screen can migrate without a full rewrite.
Deprecated APIs have owners, dates, and replacement guidance.
accessibility: Migration cannot regress existing accessible names, focus, or keyboard behavior.
responsive: Examples include narrow and wide compositions.
performance: Compare bundle and runtime cost before and after migration.
browser_notes: Migration guidance notes any changed browser baseline.

title: Document decisions, not just component screenshots
principle: For each primitive, document why it exists, when to use it, when not to use it, content rules, states, and tested behavior.
rationale: Screenshots communicate appearance but not selection criteria; without decisions, teams fork components or misuse variants.
applies_when: A component or token is shared beyond its author.
implementation: Pair API examples with usage guidance and counterexamples.
Link decisions to source rules and tests.
verification: A new contributor can choose between similar primitives without asking the author.
Documentation examples are exercised in CI or review.
accessibility: Document keyboard, focus, screen-reader, contrast, and high-contrast expectations.
responsive: Show behavior at content-driven breakpoints, not only fixed device mockups.
performance: Call out expensive options and loading strategies.
browser_notes: List tested support and known platform differences.
